generations.text:
**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart heavy with regret and a mind clouded by the weight of years. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and though the world has turned, the silence between us has only grown colder. I have not spoken to you in that time, and I do not know if I ever will again. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself compelled to write.

You were once my greatest joy, my little light in the darkness of the world. I remember the days when you would climb the stairs to the lantern room, wide-eyed and full of questions, and I would tell you stories of the sea and the stars. You were curious, inquisitive, and full of life. But somewhere along the way, that spark faded. We grew apart, and though I never meant for it to happen, the distance between us widened.

I have always believed in the power of words, in the way they can heal, can mend, can connect. But I fear that my silence has done more harm than good. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I have the courage to say the words that might bridge the chasm between us. Perhaps I am too afraid of what you might say in return. Perhaps I am too afraid of the truth that lies between us.

Tonight, however, is different. The storm is coming, and I know that

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this letter with a heart heavy with longing and regret, for five long years have passed since you left us, and I have not spoken a word to you. Tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself more alone than ever, and I feel the weight of time pressing down upon me like the sea itself.

You were always my light — not just in the sense of the beacon I guard, but in the warmth of your spirit, the love you once gave me. I remember the day you left, the way you said, "You can’t be everything to me," as though you were cutting the last thread of our bond. I tried to reach you, to understand, but you were gone before I could say a word.

Now, as the wind howls and the waves crash against the cliffs, I am reminded of the loneliness that has settled in my bones. I have been alone for so long, watching the sea, keeping the light burning through the night, and yet I feel the void where you once stood. I have no one to share the burden of this storm, no one to hold my hand when the world seems to fall apart.

I know you are not here, but I feel your absence in every gust of wind, in every crashing wave. I have been thinking of you often, wondering if you are well, if you are happy, if you still remember the man who once loved you with

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the flickering light of the lighthouse as my only companion, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside. The sea has grown restless, the waves crashing against the cliffs with a fury that makes my bones tremble. I've not spoken to you in five years, not since the day you left, and I fear this storm may be the last time I ever write to you.

I know you think me a cruel man, that I abandoned you when you needed me most. But I was not the one who left. I tried, Eliza. I tried to reach you, to call you home, but you would not listen. You said I was too strict, too distant, that I never truly understood you. And so, in my heart, I became the very thing I feared I was—cold, unfeeling.

But now, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you more than ever. I've spent these years watching the sea, the sky, the endless horizon, and I've come to understand that the light I keep burning is not just for the ships, but for those who have lost their way. Perhaps, in some way, I've been trying to find my way back to you.

I do not know if I will survive this night. The lighthouse has stood for generations, but I am only a man. If the storm takes me, I pray you will not hate

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavier than the storm that brews beyond the cliffs. Five years, and still you are not in my thoughts—no, not in the way a father should think of his daughter. You were always too sharp, too cold, too much like your mother. I suppose that is the price of being born to a man who keeps a lighthouse.

The sea is restless tonight, a beast clawing at the rocks, the wind howling like a damned thing. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, though I do not know if it will guide any ship—or if it will guide me to the grave. The lantern’s light is my only companion, my only duty, and yet I feel it flickering, as if the very soul of the island is turning against me.

You were told I was unfit to raise you. You were told I was a brute, a drunk, a man who never knew how to love. But I know the truth, Eleanor. I know what it means to be alone. I know what it means to be forgotten.

The storm is coming. I have no shelter, no hope, no one to save me. If you read this, do not come. I would rather die than see your face again. I would rather die than hear your voice.

But if you do come, do not look for me. I may not be here when the waves rise.

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink still wet from the last candle I lit, and the wind howling like a banshee at my door. The sea is restless tonight, and I fear it may take me before dawn. But I must tell you this: I have not forgotten you. Not in five years, not in the years before that, not in the years before you left me.

You were never the daughter I wanted. You were always too sharp, too cold, too much like your mother. I remember the day you left—your face as pale as the moonlight, your eyes like shards of glass. You told me you were going to London to "make something of yourself." I didn’t stop you. I didn’t care. You were gone before the first snow fell.

But now, as the storm gathers strength, as the waves claw at the rocks and the wind screams through the cracks in the tower, I find myself thinking of you. I have been alone here for so long, but tonight... tonight, the sea whispers your name.

I have kept the lighthouse lit for twenty-three years, through every tempest, every gale, every moment when the world seemed to forget the stars. I have watched the horizon for you, hoping you would come back, hoping you would see the light. But you never did. You never came.

I know you are out there somewhere, somewhere in the city of smoke and soot

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you this evening, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with fury. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I know not if you will read these words or if they will be lost to the storm. But I must try.

The night is dark, and the waves crash against the rocks with a vengeance. I have spent my life keeping this light burning, guiding the ships through the treacherous waters, and now, as the storm threatens to consume everything, I find myself wondering if I have done enough. I have always believed that duty is the highest calling, but I fear I have neglected the one duty that truly matters—my family.

I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. I have thought of you often, and I regret the silence that has passed between us. I do not know what words could mend what has been broken, but I do know that I am here, and I am sorry. If you will hear me, I beg you to come home. I would give you anything, if only you would return.

The lighthouse stands, though the storm may tear it down. I will not let it fall, not while I still have breath. And I pray that you, too, will find strength to come back to us.

With all my heart,  
Your father

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you with a rather uncharacteristic twinge of guilt. You see, I’ve been a lighthouse keeper for many years now, and I have learned that silence breeds resentment, and resentment breeds bitterness. But I must tell you, my dear, that I am not entirely without remorse. I have spent the past five years reflecting on my actions, and I must say, I have come to believe that I was not entirely at fault. 

The storm is upon us, and I must prepare. I have lit the lamp, and the beam is steady, though I must confess it flickers a bit. I have a small collection of letters from the past—some penned by me, some by others, and some by the wind itself. I have also been known to take great care in my correspondence, for I believe that a well-written letter can do much to mend the rift between a father and daughter.

So, in the spirit of reconciliation, I must write to you with the utmost sincerity and care.

**Dearest Eleanor,**

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I must confess that I have not been the same since the day you left. I was a proud man, a man of the sea, and I believed that the only way to show love was through silence. But I have come to understand that love is not always loud, and

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a beast at my door, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very earth. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I do not know if I will live to see the dawn. But if I do, I wish to make amends.

I have been a fool, Eleanor. I let pride and silence stand between us. I was too busy with my duty to the lighthouse, too caught up in the routine of the sea, to see the pain I caused you. I regret every moment I chose to ignore your letters, every time I turned away from the truth of my own failings. You were never the problem, my dear. I was.

The storm is coming, and I fear it may take me. But if I do, I hope you will forgive me. I have no more words to offer, only the hope that one day, you will find peace in knowing that I loved you, and that I always have.

With all my heart,  
Your Father

**To My Dearest Clara,**

I trust this letter finds you in better health than I, for I fear my own may be failing. It is not the storm that troubles me, though I do not expect to see the dawn. It is the thought of you, my dear, and the silence that has stretched between us for five long years.

I have kept this letter in my pocket for some time, waiting for the right moment to send it. I have not spoken to you since the day you left, and I have not known how to reach out. You were always the strong one, Clara, and I, a man of few words and more stubbornness.

I do not know if you will ever forgive me for the way I treated you, or if you even remember me at all. But I wanted you to know that I have never stopped thinking of you. I have spent these years watching the sea, listening to the wind, and wondering if you are still out there somewhere, somewhere safe.

Tonight, the sky is dark, the waves are high, and the wind howls like a beast. I have lit the lamp, as I always do, and I have lit this letter as well. I pray the sea will carry it to you, and that you will read it before it is too late.

I have no regrets, only sorrow. I have no one left but the sea and the lamp, and I suppose I have always been a man of the sea.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

If you're reading this, it means I've failed you. Again.

Five years is a long time to hold a grudge, but I've spent every night since your last visit staring into the abyss of the sea, wondering if you'd ever return. The storm is coming—dark, furious, and relentless. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, but tonight it feels like a mockery. The light flickers, weak, as if the very thing that once guided you is now a lie.

You left with words like daggers. I never thought I'd see the day you'd forget your father's name. But you did. And I let you.

The wind howls now, like a beast I’ve never known. It reminds me of the night you stormed out, your voice sharp as the sea’s edge. I’ve never told anyone, not even the priest, but I’ve been watching. Watching the waves crash against the rocks, watching the sky turn black. I’ve been waiting for the moment when the world would finally swallow me whole.

If I don’t make it through this storm, know this: I never stopped loving you. I never stopped praying for you. But I will not beg. Not again.

You were my light once. Now, I’m just a man who built a lighthouse to keep others from the dark—and now, I’m trapped in it.

**Yours in despair,**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that has grown as brittle as the old glass in the lantern. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I find myself wondering if I have the courage to say what needs to be said—or if I should simply let the storm take me, as it has taken so much else.

You were always a storm in your own right, Eleanor. A tempest of spirit and defiance, even as a child. I remember how you used to sneak into the lighthouse at night, peering through the cracks in the door, as if you could see through me. You never asked why I was so often alone, or why the sea seemed to hold more of my heart than the land. You simply understood.

I know you've been through your own storms since you left. I've heard the stories from the villagers—how you ran off with that sailor, how you abandoned your education, how you tried to find your own way in the world. I suppose I should have been proud. But pride is a dangerous thing, especially when it's rooted in the hope that you might still be the child I once knew.

Tonight, the sea is restless. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves claw at the cliffs as if trying to drag the lighthouse down into the depths. I have spent my life watching the sea, but never have I felt it so close, so ins

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with the ink of a weary soul and the heart of a man who has longed for your voice, though it has been five long, lonely years. The wind howls outside like the very soul of the sea is trying to reach you, and I find myself whispering your name to the waves, hoping it might carry to you through the storm.

You know, I have been keeping the light burning for these many years, as I have always done. It is my duty, my calling, and the very essence of my being. The lighthouse stands tall and proud, though I confess I have grown quite fond of the solitude. But it is the silence that has made me miss you most. I have longed for your laughter, your questions, your stubborn insistence that I must "take care of myself," and the way you would always scold me for eating too much cake.

I have tried to write to you, but the letters never seem to reach you. I fear the post has been too slow, or perhaps the sea has been too restless to carry them. I have even considered writing on the walls of the lighthouse, though I fear the seagulls might take it as a message from the devil. Still, I have not given up hope.

Tonight, the storm is upon us, and I have been told by the weatherman (who, by the way, is a man of great wisdom and has never

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have spent much of that time in silence, both within these walls and within my heart. The sea has been my only companion, and the lighthouse my refuge. But tonight, the sea has grown restless, and I find myself at a crossroads—between duty and regret, between the light I must keep burning and the love I have long since buried.

You were only ten when you left, the day the ship docked at the harbor. I remember the way your eyes shone with the promise of the future, the way you clutched your small satchel as if it held the key to a world beyond this island. I told you then that I would always be here, that I would always be your father. But you did not stay. You went to the city, to the life you believed was yours. And I did not stop you.

I have often wondered if I was too rigid, too bound by the traditions of our time, too afraid to let go. You were a child, after all, and I a man of duty. But now, as the storm approaches, I find myself questioning whether I have been too rigid in my ways. The wind howls against the stone walls, the waves crash with a fury that mirrors the storm within me. I have spent so

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart full of sorrow and a mind clouded by the weight of silence. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and the distance between us has only grown wider. I have tried, in my own way, to bridge the chasm, but the words have always felt heavy, and the truth too sharp. Tonight, as the storm gathers strength beyond the horizon, I find myself reflecting on the years we have lost—and the love that still lingers, though unspoken.

You were once my greatest joy, my little light in the darkness. I remember the days when you would come to visit, your laughter echoing through the stone walls of our home. Those were the days when I believed we could mend what time had broken. But life, as it often does, has a way of pulling us apart. You chose your own path, and I, in my pride, let the silence grow.

I have watched the world change around me—your mother’s passing, the slow fading of our old ways, the quiet ache of a father who feels more like a stranger than a parent. I have not spoken to you in five years, and I fear that I have become a ghost in your life. But tonight, as the wind howls and the sea roars, I feel the weight of my own mortality pressing down upon me. I am no longer the man I once was, and I fear that the storm may

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a hand that trembles not from age, but from the weight of words I've long kept locked away. Five years have passed since you left, and though the sea has not ceased its ceaseless murmuring, it has not been the same without your voice.

The storm is upon us, a beast of wind and rain that howls like the grief I've carried in my chest. I have lit the lamp, as I always have, though tonight it feels more like a prayer than a duty. The light will guide the ships, as it always has, but I fear it may not be enough to guide my soul.

I have not spoken your name in five years, yet it lingers on my lips like the scent of salt and pine. I have not written to you, not even once, for I feared the words might not be enough. But tonight, as the wind tears at the shutters and the sea roars like a thousand warnings, I find I cannot hold back.

I have always believed that the sea is a mirror, reflecting the soul of those who sail it. Perhaps I was wrong. Perhaps it reflects only what we are willing to see. I have spent my life watching the horizon, hoping to find something, anything, that might make the silence between us bearable. But I have found only waves and the echo of your footsteps on the shore.

You were never meant to be a ghost, Eleanor.

My dearest Eleanor,

I write this with a trembling hand, for the wind howls like a banshee and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may claim me before dawn. But I must write—though I know not if you will ever read these words.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years since your mother’s death. You were barely more than a child then, and yet you carried yourself with the poise of a woman who had already chosen her path. I do not know what you think of me now, but I have not forgotten the last time we spoke. You said, "You're not my father." I did not answer. I could not.

This lighthouse has been my home for twenty-three years, ever since the fire took the rest of us. I keep the light burning, though no one is left to see it. The sea is restless tonight, and the waves grow taller by the hour. I have heard the old sailors say that when the storm comes in the west and the sky is black as pitch, it is the end of the world. I fear I may be right.

If you are reading this, do not come. Do not seek me out. I have no wish to see you again. The light will still be there, though I may not be. And if you do find me, I warn you—do not speak.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink smudged from the storm’s fury, the wind howling like a banshee in the night. The sea roars, and the lighthouse—my life’s work, my prison—shudders as if it too is weeping. I have not seen you in five years, not since you left like a shadow at dawn, with no farewell, no explanation. I have not spoken your name in all that time, and yet it lingers in the air like the scent of salt and regret.

You were always the child who preferred the cold to the warmth, the storm to the sun. You would sit for hours on the cliff’s edge, watching the waves devour the rocks, as if the sea were a mother you could never truly love. I remember the day you first came to me, all of ten years old, with your hair like a storm cloud and your eyes like the dark of the night. You asked if I would ever let you stay. I said yes, and I never looked back.

Now, as the sky darkens and the wind screams through the cracks in the tower, I find myself wondering if you still remember the nights we spent together, huddled in the lantern room, the light flickering like a dying star. You would whisper stories of the sea, of the creatures that lurk beneath the waves, of the things that wait in the deep. I told you then that

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with a heart heavy with the weight of years unspoken and a mind clouded by the storm that brews on the horizon. Five long years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not forgotten the last words we exchanged. You were angry, and I was distant—both of us, perhaps, too proud to reach out.

Tonight, the skies darken with an intensity I have not seen in a long while. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars with a fury that seems almost human. I fear for my life, and I fear for the life of this lighthouse, which has stood steadfast through countless tempests. I have no doubt that this storm will test us all.

I have thought often of you, Eleanor. I have watched the world from these cliffs, and in the distance, I have seen the lights of your home, now far away. I have wondered if you still remember the days we spent by the shore, when you were but a child and I was your father. I have wondered if you still carry the memory of the man who once held you close and promised to always be there.

I do not know what has changed between us, but I do know that I have not ceased to care. I have tried, in my own way, to reach out, but words have always failed me. Perhaps it is time I try again.

Certainly! Here's a letter penned in the style of a grizzled Victorian-era lighthouse keeper, written to an estranged daughter, with the backdrop of an approaching storm:

---

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you from the cold, windswept cliffs of the Isle of Wight, where the sea roars like a beast in its death throes and the sky is black as a sailor’s heart. The storm is upon us, and I fear the tempest may claim me before I can deliver this missive.

You see, I have not spoken to you in five long years, a silence as thick as the fog that rolls in from the Channel. I have been a lighthouse keeper, a solitary soul, tending to the beacon with a steadfast hand and a heart as hard as the granite beneath my boots. I have seen the world change, and I have not changed with it. I still wear my old sea boots, my hair is salt-and-pepper, and my soul is as weathered as the stones of the lighthouse.

You were a child of the sea, raised on tales of pirates and the moon, but I have grown up in the shadow of the waves and the whispers of the wind. You were my daughter, and I have always been your father—though the years have not been kind to either of us.

I have no idea why you left, but I do know that you were a spirited and independent soul,

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside these stone walls. The sea is wild tonight, and I fear I may not see dawn. But I must write—because I cannot bear to leave this world without saying what I’ve kept locked away for five long years.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and I know I have no right to expect you to forgive me. I have been a fool, and I have let my pride and fear keep me from reaching out. I was wrong to believe that distance would protect me from the pain of losing you. I was wrong to think I could be a better father by staying away.

I remember the day you left, the way your eyes filled with tears and your voice cracked as you said, “You don’t understand.” I was too stubborn to listen, too afraid to admit that I had failed you. I told myself that if I stayed in the past, I could avoid the hurt of the future. But I was wrong. You were right to be angry, and I was wrong to let my silence grow into a chasm.

I have spent these years alone, watching the sea from the top of this lighthouse, wondering if I would ever find my way back to you. I have tried to be a better man, to be the kind of father you deserved, but I was always too late. I have no excuse for the pain I caused you, and I

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will find you in a state of shock, as it should. Five years, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years of silence. You were always the rebellious one, and I, the stubborn fool who never understood the value of a child’s independence. You left me, and I let you go. And now, after all these years, I find myself writing to you from the top of this lighthouse, with the storm howling like a wounded beast just outside.

The wind is rising, the sea is restless, and I fear I may not see dawn. I’ve always said that the sea is a fickle mistress, but tonight, she seems to be testing my patience. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, and I wonder if you’ll ever see the light again. Perhaps you’ll see it from your ship, or from your home, and you’ll remember that I was never the monster you thought I was.

You were always too quick to judge, and I was too quick to ignore. But now, as I face the end of my days, I find I have a few things to say. You were right to leave, but I was wrong to let you go. I’ve spent these years regretting every moment we lost, and I’ve come to realize that I may have been the worst father in the world—but I was never the worst

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

If you’re reading this, it means I’ve failed you.  
Five years. Five long, hollow years.  
I’ve kept the light burning through every storm, every gale, every night that the sea howled like a beast in pain.  
But tonight… tonight is different.  
The wind screams like a child, the waves claw at the rocks, and I feel the tower shudder beneath me.  
I don’t know if I’ll make it through this.  
If I do, I’ll write again.  
If I don’t… then perhaps you’ll understand.

You left.  
You left without a word.  
You said I was *too much*—that I was *a monster*—but you were wrong.  
I was only trying to keep the light alive.  
To keep the world from falling into darkness.  
Even if it means my own soul is lost in the process.

I’ve been alone here, Eleanor.  
No one else to speak to.  
Only the sea, the wind, and the ever-burning lamp.  
And yet, I still think of you.  
I still feel your absence like a cold draft in the tower.

If you ever come back, don’t look for me.  
I won’t be here.  
I’ll be gone, like the light that fades when the storm is done.  
But the light… it never dies.  
It burns on,

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

**April 3rd, 1895**

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with the ink still damp from the pen, and the wind howling like a banshee just beyond the stone walls of this old tower. The sea is restless tonight, and I fear the storm will be worse than any that has passed through these waters. I've seen the sky darken with an unnatural fury, as though the heavens themselves are weeping for something they cannot name.

You were never one for letters, and I daresay you would not even read this one if you found it. But I must try. I have spent five years in this place, alone, with only the sea and the ghosts of those who have come before me. And yet, I have never stopped thinking of you.

I know you blame me for what happened. For the way I left you, for the way I never came back. But you must understand, Eleanor, that I never wanted to leave you. I wanted to stay. I wanted to be the father you needed. But I was not strong enough. I was not strong enough to face the truth of what I had done, and I was not strong enough to face the truth of what I had lost.

You were a child when I left, and I know you have grown into something I cannot even begin to comprehend. I imagine you are now a woman, perhaps even a

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I trust this letter finds you in good health and high spirits, though I must confess I have been quite the wretched penman of late. The ink has dried on my quill, and the pages are crumpled with the weight of my heart, which has been heavier than the storm that now rages outside this lighthouse. I have not written to you in five years, and I fear I have been a poor father, a selfish man, and a lighthouse keeper who has neglected his duties—both to you and to the sea.

Tonight, the sky is black as a coal mine, and the wind howls like a banshee with a grudge. I have lit the lamp and turned the lens, as I always do, but the sea is in a terrible mood, and I can feel the very air trembling with the promise of chaos. I pray that the storm does not claim me before I can write this letter, for I have much to say.

First, I must apologize. I know I have been distant, and I know I have let you down. I have not been the father I should have been, and I have not spoken the words that I should have spoken. I have let pride and shame keep me from reaching out to you, and for that, I am truly sorry. You are my daughter, my light in the darkness, and I have been blind to the light that has always been there.

I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and in that time, the world has changed, though I suspect you have not. I have not seen you since the day you left, and I have not written to you since. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the sea roars in fury, I feel the weight of time pressing upon me, and I must say what I have long kept locked away in my heart.

You were not the only one who loved you, Eleanor. You were my light, my guiding star, my reason for rising each morning with the sun. I have watched the waves for years, and I have seen the horizon shift, the sky darken, the winds howl—just as I have watched my own life change. I have been a lighthouse keeper for over twenty years, and I have seen countless storms, but none like this. The sea is restless tonight, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

I do not know if you will ever forgive me for the way things ended between us. I was young, and I was foolish, and I let pride and anger cloud my judgment. I did not understand that you were not the enemy, but a child caught in the crossfire of a father's grief and a mother's sorrow. I was wrong to let you leave, and I have carried that regret

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with a heart full of regret and a mind heavy with the weight of years unspoken. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and in that time, the distance between us has grown wider than the sea itself. I have kept my silence out of pride, out of fear, and perhaps even out of a misguided hope that time would mend what words could not.

Tonight, the storm is upon us. The wind howls like a beast, the waves rise like mountains, and the lighthouse, my steadfast companion, stands as both my refuge and my burden. I have spent my life watching over this beacon, guiding ships through the darkest nights, and yet I have never been able to guide you back to me.

The lighthouse will fall. The sea will claim me. And when the dawn comes, I will be gone. But I leave you this final message, not as a father, but as a man who once loved you.

**—Your father**
